articles.title: The AI Due Diligence Gap
articles.excerpt: Most PE due diligence processes treat AI as a technology question. It is a financial one. Here is what institutional investors are missing — and what it is costing them at close.
articles.body:
When a PE fund evaluates an acquisition target today, the due diligence workstream typically covers financials, legal, commercial, IT, and — increasingly — AI. But here is the problem: in most processes, AI is treated as a technology question. It belongs under the IT workstream. Someone checks whether the company "uses AI," what tools it has deployed, and whether there is a data science team.

That is the wrong lens. AI is a financial question. And treating it otherwise is costing investors money at close and during the hold period.

## The gap is not adoption — it is assessment.

The PE industry is not ignoring AI. According to Pictet's 2024 survey, nearly two-thirds of general partners are running GenAI pilots, and over 40% have embedded it in business processes. PwC's benchmark testing shows productivity gains of 35–85% in certain diligence tasks. McKinsey's 2026 Global Private Markets Report notes that leading GPs are now reflecting AI upside and downside directly in IC materials, diligence, and value creation plans.

The adoption curve is not the issue. The issue is that most diligence processes still evaluate AI as infrastructure — not as a financial lever with measurable impact on EBITDA, cost-income ratios, and exit multiples.

## What institutional investors are actually missing.

There are three blind spots I see repeatedly:

**First, AI is not stress-tested against the value creation plan.** A target company may claim that AI drives 15% of revenue or a 20% reduction in processing costs. But diligence rarely verifies whether those gains are sustainable, attributable to AI specifically, or dependent on a key vendor relationship that introduces concentration risk. If a portfolio company's AI-driven efficiency is actually a single automation built on a third-party API with no contractual lock-in, that is not a competitive moat — it is a dependency.

**Second, data quality is assumed, not audited.** MIT's 2025 State of AI report found that the vast majority of enterprise GenAI pilots fail to deliver measurable P&L impact — and a primary driver is data: dirty inputs, fragmented systems, and no governance. If due diligence does not independently audit the quality, provenance, and legal ownership of the data feeding AI systems, the investment thesis is built on an unverified assumption.

**Third, regulatory exposure is underweighted.** By August 2026, the EU AI Act classifies AI-driven credit scoring, loan approval, and insurance risk assessment as high-risk — with penalties of up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover. For a mid-sized European bank with €10 billion in turnover, a 7% fine is €700 million — board-level material. If a target company operates in the EU, or serves EU customers, and uses AI in any of these areas, the compliance liability is a material financial risk that belongs in the valuation model, not in a footnote.

## The financial consequence is real.

Industry analyses building on RAND Corporation data show AI projects failing at rates approaching 80% overall — with financial services often cited among the highest — and failed initiatives frequently costing millions per project. At the portfolio level, across 8–15 companies, the compounding effect of AI mispricing is significant. And with median PE purchase multiples at 11.8x EBITDA in 2025, according to McKinsey's Global Private Markets Report — the highest in recent years — overpaying for AI capabilities that do not survive scrutiny is an expensive mistake.

Meanwhile, S&P Global analysis shows 42% of companies scrapped most of their AI initiatives in 2025, up from 17% the year before. That is not a marginal correction. That is a market-wide repricing of what AI actually delivers versus what was promised.

## What I see from both sides of the table.

I have the unusual position of sitting on both sides of this question. As senior advisor for CVC Capital Partners, I supported portfolio value optimisation and acquisition diligence in financial services — evaluating AI claims from the investor's chair. Right now at TDB Bank, I am leading a large Temenos core banking transformation with 250+ staff, deploying AI in a regulated banking environment with real compliance obligations, real data challenges, and real P&L accountability.

That dual lens is why I built Digit-AI around a specific proposition: AI diligence is not a technology audit. It is a financial assessment that should feed your IC memo, not your IT appendix.

## What a rigorous AI diligence process should include.

The diligence workstream should assess: data quality, provenance, and legal ownership. Model architecture, technical debt, and vendor dependency. Regulatory compliance across the EU AI Act, GDPR, and sector-specific obligations. Competitive moat durability — whether AI advantages are defensible or replicable. And, critically, whether the AI-driven economics in the management presentation survive independent validation.

The output should be a risk-adjusted valuation assessment — not a maturity score. A board- and IC-ready financial case, not a technology benchmarking exercise.

Digit-AI runs a 3-week AI Value & Defensibility Audit that quantifies EBITDA/efficiency upside and governance risk — then can stay on as Fractional CAIO to deploy the top 2–3 opportunities. The assessment feeds directly into the investment committee memo, because the question for institutional investors is no longer whether to adopt AI — but whether it is actually changing the economics, and whether you have verified that before signing.

articles.title: Why Your AI Strategy Is Probably a Wish List
articles.excerpt: A real AI strategy is constrained by data maturity, talent capacity, and regulatory reality. Most are not. The gap between ambition and economics is where value gets destroyed.
articles.body:
Every bank and large enterprise I speak with has an AI strategy. Most of them are wish lists.

They have the right ambition: reduce cost-income ratios, automate manual processes, improve decision speed. They reference the right technologies. They have executive sponsorship — or at least an executive who signed off on the budget.

But they are not strategies. They are aspiration documents disguised as plans. And the gap between aspiration and economics is where value gets destroyed.

## The numbers tell the story.

MIT's NANDA initiative studied enterprise AI deployments in 2025 and found that only about 5% of GenAI pilots achieved measurable P&L impact or rapid revenue acceleration; the vast majority stall, delivering little to no measurable business value. Separately, S&P Global analysis found 42% of companies scrapped most of their AI initiatives in 2025 — more than double the rate of the previous year. And BCG's research found that 74% of companies have yet to show any tangible value from their AI investments.

These are not edge cases. This is the market norm. And the root cause is not technology.

## The three constraints that separate a strategy from a wish list.

A real AI strategy is constrained. A wish list is not. The difference comes down to three things most organisations skip:

**Data maturity.** Every AI system depends on data, and the data is almost never ready. MIT's report concluded that the dominant barrier to scaling GenAI is not infrastructure, regulation, or talent — it is learning from enterprise data realities. Systems that do not adapt stall in pilot mode. When I deployed digital and AI capabilities at Standard Chartered across 60 markets, data quality, provenance, and governance were the hardest problems. Not the algorithms. If your strategy assumes the data is clean and accessible, it is a wish list.

**Talent capacity.** McKinsey reports that nearly two-thirds of organisations remain stuck in pilot mode, unable to scale across the enterprise. A primary reason is that most companies plan ambitious AI programmes and then discover they lack the internal capability to execute. The MIT study found that specialised vendor-led projects succeed more often than pure internal builds. If your strategy depends entirely on hiring a data science team that does not yet exist, it is a wish list.

**Regulatory reality.** Nobody thinks about the regulator until month 8. I have seen this pattern repeatedly. The technology works. The business case is approved. Then the compliance team asks for model risk documentation, audit trails, and human oversight protocols that were never built in. At TDB Bank, where I lead a $19M transformation right now, compliance signs off before anything goes live. That is not a constraint — it is a design principle. If your strategy does not include regulatory approvals as milestones, it is a wish list.

## The budget allocation problem.

MIT research also highlighted a common misallocation: organisations frequently pour budget into high-visibility sales and marketing tools, while the highest ROI consistently appears in back-office automation — eliminating business process outsourcing, reducing external agency costs, and streamlining operational workflows.

This is a familiar pattern. Organisations invest where the narrative is exciting rather than where the economics are strongest. The result: expensive pilots in high-visibility functions that never scale, while the operational improvements that would actually change the cost-income ratio go unfunded.

Successful organisations do the opposite. The small percentage that deliver results focus on fewer use cases, invest heavily in foundations — data, governance, change management — and have clear pre-approval metrics tied to financial outcomes.

## What a real AI strategy looks like.

It starts with an honest assessment of where you are — not where you wish you were. What is the actual state of your data? What talent do you have today, not in 18 months? What will the regulator approve, and how long will that take?

Then it prioritises ruthlessly. Not 15 pilots across 12 departments. Two or three use cases where the financial case is clear, the data exists, the regulatory pathway is understood, and the organisation has the capacity to execute.

At Standard Chartered, the $800M digital transformation I led across 60 markets succeeded not because we had more AI ambition than other banks — but because we connected strategy to operating reality. We digitised $3.7 billion in transaction banking revenue, delivering a significant revenue uplift, through disciplined execution in specific functions where the economics justified the investment and the regulatory pathway was clear.

That is exactly the approach we apply at Digit-AI: constrain ambition to what will actually get approved, implemented, adopted, and valued — starting with a 3-week assessment that quantifies the real opportunity, not the theoretical one.

The organisations that win with AI are not the ones with the most ambitious strategies. They are the ones with the most honest ones.

articles.title: The EU AI Act — What Boards Should Actually Worry About
articles.excerpt: The EU AI Act is not an IT compliance exercise. It is a board-level governance obligation with material liability attached — and the penalties exceed the GDPR.
articles.body:
On 2 August 2026, the remaining provisions of the EU AI Act become enforceable. For most boards, this date is still treated as an IT compliance deadline. It is not. It is a governance obligation with material financial liability attached — and the penalties exceed the GDPR.

Here is what executives and board members need to understand before their next meeting.

## The headline numbers.

The EU AI Act imposes a tiered penalty structure based on the severity of the infringement. For prohibited AI practices — such as social scoring systems or certain biometric applications — the maximum fine is €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher. For non-compliance with high-risk system obligations, fines reach €15 million or 3% of global turnover. For providing incorrect or misleading information to regulators, penalties go up to €7.5 million or 1.5% of turnover.

For context: 7% of global revenue would represent approximately $8.5 billion for Meta, $14 billion for Google, and $16 billion for Microsoft based on 2024 financials. For a mid-sized European bank with €10 billion in turnover, a 7% fine is €700 million — board-level material. These are not theoretical figures. They are the regulatory ceiling now embedded in EU law.

## What the Act actually requires.

The EU AI Act is the first comprehensive legal framework for AI anywhere in the world. It entered into force in August 2024, with a phased implementation timeline. Prohibited AI practices have been enforceable since February 2025. General-purpose AI obligations have applied since August 2025. And the full weight of high-risk system requirements takes effect in August 2026.

The Act uses a risk-based classification. Most AI systems fall into the minimal-risk category and are largely unaffected. The regulatory burden concentrates on high-risk systems — and this is where financial services boards need to pay attention.

## Why financial services is directly in scope.

The Act's Annex III explicitly classifies several AI applications common in banking and insurance as high-risk:

- AI systems used for credit scoring and creditworthiness assessment
- AI systems for risk assessment and pricing in life and health insurance
- AI used in recruitment, employee evaluation, and workforce management
- AI systems for automated decision-making that affects access to essential services

For any bank running AI-powered credit decisions, any insurer using algorithmic pricing, or any financial institution deploying AI in loan origination, these are not theoretical categories. These are systems already in production — and by August 2026, they are subject to mandatory risk management frameworks, technical documentation, human oversight requirements, transparency obligations, and conformity assessments.

The EBA's own mapping exercise, completed in late 2025, confirmed that AI systems for credit scoring in the banking and payments sector are classified as high-risk, requiring structured risk management, quality management, and human supervision.

## The board-level governance obligation.

This is the point most boards have not fully absorbed: the EU AI Act elevates AI governance to an explicit board-level responsibility. Directors face potential personal liability under corporate law fiduciary duties if they consciously disregard significant AI-related regulatory risks.

This means the board needs to be able to answer several questions: which AI systems in the organisation are classified as high-risk? Are conformity assessments complete? Is there a documented risk management system for each? Is human oversight built into the operational process — not just described in policy documents? Are there clear accountability lines for AI governance, and do they reach the board?

If the answer to any of these is "we are not sure," the governance gap is already a liability.

## The Digital Omnibus complication.

In late 2025, the European Commission proposed the Digital Omnibus package, which could postpone certain high-risk obligations for Annex III systems until December 2027. This proposal is currently under legislative review by the European Parliament and Council.

Some organisations are treating this as a reason to delay preparation. That is a mistake. The Omnibus proposal has not been formally adopted. Even if it passes, the extension applies only to specific categories, and the broader enforcement infrastructure — national competent authorities, the EU AI Office, the AI Board — is already operational. Prudent boards plan for August 2026, not hoping for December 2027.

## What I see from inside a regulated bank.

At TDB Bank, where I currently serve as First Deputy CEO, I lead AI deployment inside a regulated environment with real compliance obligations, real regulatory relationships, and real consequences for non-compliance.

The governance framework I apply there — compliance sign-off before anything goes live, model risk documentation built into the deployment from week one, human-in-the-loop triggers calibrated to risk appetite — is not a theoretical framework. It is the operating standard I use every day with 250+ staff and 1M+ clients depending on the platform.

When I previously led the $800M digital transformation at Standard Chartered across 60 markets, the same principle applied: regulatory approval was not an afterthought bolted on in month 8. It was a design requirement from the start. That rigour — building governance into the architecture rather than layering it on after deployment — is what separates organisations that ship AI in regulated environments from those that stall at the pilot stage.

## What boards should do now.

These are the five things I would put on the board agenda:

**First, conduct an AI inventory.** Identify every AI system in the organisation, classify it against the Act's risk categories, and determine whether it falls under high-risk.

**Second, assign governance accountability.** AI compliance cannot sit solely with the CTO or the compliance team. It requires explicit board-level oversight with clear reporting lines.

**Third, start conformity assessments now.** Technical documentation, risk management systems, and human oversight protocols take months to build properly. Waiting for the deadline guarantees non-compliance.

**Fourth, review vendor contracts.** If the organisation deploys third-party AI systems, it is still a deployer under the Act — with its own set of obligations around monitoring, incident reporting, and human oversight. The vendor's compliance does not absolve the deployer.

**Fifth, do not plan for the extension.** The Digital Omnibus may or may not pass in its current form. The board's risk posture should be based on the enforceable date, not the proposed one.

The EU AI Act is not an IT project. It is a board-level governance obligation with penalties that exceed the GDPR and personal liability implications for directors. Treating it as anything less than that is, itself, a governance failure.
